# Chapter 8 Collaborating on Investigations


<aside class="learning-objectives c3 main-flow v1" epub:type="learning-objectives" id="bra_9781319362065_AfELAT3p7g" title="Chapter Objectives">

## Chapter Objectives

*In this chapter you will learn how to:*

- Identify successful examples of collaborations in investigative journalism.
- Select, plan and carry out effective collaborations.
- Apply standards used for collaboration by respected global organizations.

</aside>

More than four decades ago, Investigative Reporters and Editors --- an organization built to bring investigative journalists together --- conducted one of its highest profile collaborations in investigative reporting history. Known as the Arizona Project, nearly 40 reporters from about two dozen news organizations banded together in 1976 after investigative reporter and IRE member Don Bolles was murdered in Phoenix, Arizona. The group set out to extend the work Bolles had been doing on organized crime and public corruption there. The result was a 23-part series that appeared in newspapers and on news stations throughout the U.S. in 1977 and resulted not only in illuminating the depth of corruption in Phoenix but also pointed to those that conspired to kill Bolles.

Editors at some of the larger newspapers that did not participate derided the project as a personal crusade of the journalists. But the Arizona Project demonstrated that investigative reporters and editors from multiple and competing newsrooms could produce outstanding investigative work. Furthermore, groups of reporters and news organizations became so inspired by the Arizona Project that they formed similar teams to investigate the killing of reporters who had been slain because of their journalistic work. These investigations included projects on reporters murdered in California, the Mideast and Brazil.

That IRE would set the standard for such work made sense because IRE was created to connect and train investigative journalists to aid them in doing better and more thorough stories. The organization was the outcome of journalists in the 1960s and early 1970s sharing tips and information at a time in which the forms of communication were largely telephones and hard-copy letters.

Now, the idea of investigative journalists cooperating on stories has become so routine that it is often an expected approach to substantial projects. In a time of budget and staff cuts in the newspaper industry, the rise of smaller nonprofit investigative centers and the increasing globalization of stories, collaboration extended the resources and abilities of journalists to investigate complex and sometimes cross-border corruption.

In addition, as more information has gone digital, databases or sets of documents have become a key component of collaborations. Documents or data that can be sliced into subsets by geography or topic allow journalists from different regions and newsrooms to work from the same baseline.

For example, the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists (ICIJ) has shared and used sets of documents and databases on privatization of drinking water in certain areas, the dumping of asbestos into developing countries, and illegal and destructive industrial fishing. The Consortium also has taken advantage of information leaks of data on banking records, offshore accounts used to evade taxes and hide stolen public funds, and available government data on faulty medical devices. Its investigations have included as many as 400 journalists and 100 newsrooms.

Similarly, the broadcast news show "Frontline" and the Center for Investigative Reporting (CIR) produced stories together for decades on topics including handguns and corrupting political donations. One of their collaborations, an investigation called "Rape in the Fields," uncovered the sexual exploitation of female migrant workers throughout the U.S. CIR has also done an international story with several other partners on the relationship between the carbon trading market and climate change. CIR later developed a radio show, "Reveal," that partners with newsrooms across the U.S. to produce investigative stories such as these in podcast form.

But investigative collaboration extends beyond U.S. borders. In Europe, journalists have worked on cross-border collaborations including issues like misuse of farm subsidies, human trafficking and the use of dangerous pesticides. European journalists have also joined with citizens by using crowdsourcing to gather more anecdotal information to expose wrongdoing.

Another national investigative journalism organization, The Center for Public Integrity, not only formed the ICIJ in 1997, but also partnered with new nonprofit newsrooms that began to be created in 2009. In 2003, the Global Investigative Journalism Network was created to bring together nonprofit newsrooms around the world and became a vehicle for journalists worldwide to find and assist each other on cross-border stories.

Clearly, collaboration has become so much the norm that in the U.S., nonprofit newsrooms have gone so far as to create temporary collaboration teams that have worked on stories about topics like dangers at regional airports and sexual assaults on campuses. This collaborative work allows journalists to see the scope of a wider phenomenon than just in their own community.

Taking the collaborative strategy one step further, one relatively small nonprofit, The Marshall Project --- which investigates the judicial system --- has increased its number of stories and its distribution by partnering with both longstanding and startup newsrooms. "The Marshall Project aspires to partner nearly all of its work with media partners," said Andrew Bard Epstein, who served as The Marshall Project's communications and development manager until recently. "We've now co-published with over 110 different outlets since our founding four years ago. In many cases, we seek out high-profile, legacy institutions that can draw the largest possible national audience to an investigative feature. In other instances, a regional or daily newspaper may be more strategic to make real impact." In many cases, investigative collaborations have also included professors and researchers from multiple universities.

It's clear that there is no one kind of collaboration, which can range from simple to complex. Collaborations break down into many categories, including:

- Partnerships between two news organizations or two reporters from different newsrooms.
- Collaborations of three or more newsrooms or reporters in one country.
- Collaborations among several newsrooms or individuals across borders.
- Collaborations with newsrooms and university investigative centers.
- Collaborations with the public using crowdsourcing.

Journalists should keep in mind some basic tips for any collaboration, especially in the planning stages of getting a collaboration started.


## Getting Started

Before deciding to collaborate, journalists should consider or plan for the following:

- Decide whether the collaboration is truly needed. Every collaboration brings with it complications such as making sure there are similar journalism standards and potential personality differences.
- Decide who will lead and coordinate the effort. Partners in collaborations often have other duties or stories to do. A collaboration needs a leader who will keep partners focused on the project and deadlines, as well as decide others' roles. Be clear on what each partner can and will bring to the story, whether it is editorial content, data analysis, a particular media format (audio, video, etc.) or a distribution method. Identify the strengths and weaknesses of each partner at the beginning. These include experience, skills, resources and unique knowledge.
- Bring every partner in at the beginning of a collaboration. It may be necessary to add a partner during the investigation, but try to avoid that to minimize the amount of catching up to do.
- Set up a routine of weekly communication on progress and information collected.
- Determine the security level needed to protect communication, and set up that system at the outset.
- Make sure to avoid duplication of efforts.
- Decide who will do legal review of the work.
- Decide who will do follow-up stories.
- Have a graceful exit strategy for those who cannot complete the work or cannot meet the standards during the project. That strategy should include a written memo of understanding or agreement that outlines expectations and standards, who will decide if those are not being met, and emphasizes that confidential information will not be shared.

ProPublica, a nonprofit newsroom created in 2009, has done hundreds of investigations involving many partners. In an IRE Journal article about early lessons learned, Executive Editor Stephen Engelberg writes: "At their core, collaborations are about building trust. Reporters trained to compete fiercely for every scrap of news have a natural suspicion of outsiders. We have found that initial phases are crucial in making a collaboration work." Engelberg adds that, if possible, the first meeting to discuss a joint project should take place in person. "Conference calls are a lousy way to launch a relationship. While newsrooms have become much more open to change, there is an understandable skepticism about working with outsiders."

Engelberg also says it is expected that all information gathered through a joint effort belongs to the collaboration: "We don't take information from one partner and give it to another." He also notes that things don't always get better when they're rewritten in a collaboration, "but failed drafts contain valuable clues to what's going wrong, from holes in the reporting to misconceptions about what story we're trying to tell." He concludes: "Collaborative stories will take some time to hammer out. Keep on overcommunicating."


## Cross-Border and Cross-Continent Investigations

The newsroom Eldiario.es's investigative reporting project "The Enslaved Land" combined journalists and newsrooms in Europe, Africa and South America to collaborate on a project about the exploitation of poorly paid workers in undeveloped countries to produce crops for developed nations. It described the project with this headline: "This is how poor countries are used to feed rich countries."

According to an article in IJNet, nearly two dozen journalists uncovered "the hidden abuses behind five crops consumed widely in Europe (sugar, cacao, bananas, coffee and African palm) that are produced from exploitative, slave-like conditions for workers, illegal business practices and sustained environmental damage." While Europe benefited from the abusive practices, the journalists concentrated on four countries: Guatemala, Honduras, Colombia and the Ivory Coast.

According to the IJNet review:

> The investigation's findings included how powerful sugar baron families in Guatemala have evaded taxes thanks to their use of offshore companies and powerful political connections; how the monoculture of African palm in Honduras is destroying the environment while authorities turn a blind eye to federal laws; how Colombian coffee farmers are at the mercy of speculation; how a dark political network controls the distribution of land for banana farming in Colombia; and how child workers sustain the cacao trade in the Ivory Coast, a country which supplies 40 percent of the world's cacao.

In an effort to expand the story's reach and get reporting on the ground in El Salvador, Eldiario.es also allied itself with the Salvadoran independent news site El Faro. It took a year to produce a series of stories that included an examination of international trade databases and reporting in the field. "We believe that you go out and do journalism after debating a lot about what kind of journalism you want to do, and combining Eldiario.es's work with the information we were collecting was enriching for everyone. When a project is very well-defined from the beginning and both parties accept what exactly you'll be covering and how, then everything else is just conversations that any other regular newsroom would have," said Raúl Sánchez González, of Eldiario.es.

In an interview with IJNet, González said the biggest challenge was putting together two cultures and two different ways of working. But one news organization, El Faro, did on-the-ground reporting and had excellent human sources, and Eldiario.es did data analysis. On this point, Daniel Valencia, an editor at El Faro, said, "I think independent journalism is betting on collaboration. It's not the first time we've worked like this, and the results have always been successful in terms of impact, audience reach and producing an in-depth investigation ... The 'bad guys' aren't just in one country, they've gone global."

### Establishing Standards

To successfully collaborate on any scale, it's important for all working journalists to understand the standards to which their collaborations will be held. As an example, The Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project (OCCRP), based in Bosnia, has worked for years on investigative stories with other news organizations. In one collaboration, the OCCRP partnered with the Danish national newspaper Berlingske and other newsrooms to expose money laundering being committed by Azerbaijan political leaders. The stories were based on records from Dansk Bank that were leaked to Berlingske journalists and involved newsrooms in 16 countries. The stories showed that \$2.9 billion connected to the country was siphoned away through European companies and banks.

To ensure its credibility with other newsrooms and with the public, OCCRP went on to establish published standards for itself. The standards included the need for fairness, accuracy, clarity in presentation, depth in reporting and the understanding that the newsroom is providing a public service. The standards also emphasize the need for acting ethically, such as not accepting gifts, avoiding advocacy and maintaining independence for funding.

### Dealing With Leaks

Collaboration becomes more challenging when a nonjournalistic organization, particularly an advocacy organization, wants to partner. For example, in a different form of collaboration, WikiLeaks, a website devoted to publishing secret government documents, initially leaked documents and data from the U.S. military without attempts at verifying the truth of the documents or protecting confidential sources. After facing much criticism, the organization joined with several longtime and respected news organizations to help build its credibility and receive wider distributions through news stories. Writing about collaborations, Carlos Martinez de la Serna of the Tow Center at Columbia University said, "The partnership between WikiLeaks and legacy organizations was of a different nature. As described by the French news site Mediapart, it was 'a reflection of the new alliance formed by the digital revolution between professionals and amateurs, journalists and activists, news professionals and citizen whistleblowers.' " Martinez de la Serna noted, "This arrangement combines the strengths of the network with the reporting tradition. It also consolidated the presence of new computer expertise and tools in legacy newsrooms that had been building up since the 1990s, in this case to develop a secure infrastructure to organize, secure, analyze, and share large databases of documents."

But in the end, the solution to the collaboration proved to be similar to how journalists have dealt with leakers and whistleblowers: Verify the leaked documents and data.

### The ICIJ Approach

As noted earlier in the chapter, ICIJ has overseen and conducted some of the most complex global projects, especially in terms of the numbers of journalists, newsrooms and countries involved. Because of that, the Consortium developed methods for selecting partners and an agreement for maintaining trust, confidentiality and standards.

While any collaboration can be difficult, on a global level the hurdles for collaborations can be even greater. "There are plenty of challenges, to be sure --- financing these complex, multinational investigations, working in different cultures, with different languages and, sometimes, different standards," said David Kaplan, who directed the ICIJ and then went on to become executive director of the Global Investigative Journalism Network.

In order to maintain standards that can hold up to these challenges, Deputy Editor at ICIJ Marina Walker says the newsroom follows a routine procedure for deciding who can join the Consortium to work on an investigative project. Walker adds that the Consortium thoroughly examines a reporter's track record before it invites the reporter to collaborate with ICIJ. Among the questions it asks as it reviews the reporter:

- What work has the reporter produced?
- What has been written about the journalist's work?
- What do colleagues say about the reporter's journalism and ethics?
- Has the journalist's work been recognized in articles or awards?

Walker said the same questions also apply to partner organizations before they're invited to join ICIJ.

When the editors from ICIJ meet in person with reporters and partner newsrooms at the beginning of each collaboration, they use the opportunity to talk about standards of journalism as part of their strategy sessions. "For example," Walker says, "as we coordinate requests for comment we make sure that everyone understands that giving enough time for people \[subjects of investigations\] to answer questions is crucial." Walker also says ICIJ provides hands-on training on journalism techniques during the investigations. "Again," she says, "we infuse those 'how to' sessions with standards that are important to us. In sessions like those, reporters have asked our views about doing hidden cameras, for example. We have helped them find other ways to report the story every time possible."

Walker adds that ICIJ also posts guidelines for reporters on the communication platform, but those are specific to each investigation. Furthermore, she said that at the beginning of an investigation, everyone --- both reporters and organizations --- signs an agreement that outlines the principles that will guide the collaboration. The agreement includes such requirements as:

- The work products or analyses provided by the journalist or newsroom to ICIJ will not be shared or copied to any third party or published in any form unless the director or deputy director gives permission in writing.
- ICIJ will be prominently and fully credited in any publications or mentioned early in any audio or video narrative and be in the end credits.
- The reporter or newsroom understands the project is mutually cooperative and that all team members are required to share work products and analyses.
- Reporters or newsrooms communicate well and often and agree to inform ICIJ and other partners promptly about any significant findings.
- The timing of approaching major subjects and when to publish or air will be decided by ICIJ in consultation with partners.
- Anyone the partner brings in to work on the project is subject to the agreement and will be bound to the same terms and answerable to ICIJ as the overall project leader.
- ICIJ agrees that whatever stories the reporter or newsroom decides to publish will remain within that reporter's or newsroom's editorial control and that it is their responsibility to get the facts right and to ensure that all the correct legal processes are followed.

These requirements ensure that all collaborators start off the project on the same page and have a definitive framework in which their work can reside.


## Collaborations Between Journalists and Universities

Collaborations between journalists and experts at universities have been increasing as the investigative work has gone beyond simply interviewing an expert professor on an investigation's findings. Often, the journalist will ask the professor to help with the investigative methodology as it progresses, typically in a special field such as testing for lead poisoning. In one example, The Chicago Tribune used the medical expertise of professors at Columbia University and the University of Arizona to identify fatal drug interactions. The project was focused on how pharmacies in Illinois did not identify those drug interactions when filling prescriptions.

In another example, in an internal collaboration at Stanford University, journalism professors worked with the engineering department to create a comprehensive data set on racial profiling by police during traffic stops. The study found that while blacks and Hispanics were more likely to be searched, those searches were less likely to uncover illegal drugs or weapons. The Stanford team of graduate students and an assistant professor of management science and engineering developed a new measurement called a threshold test that was a more rigorous way to quantify searches.

Taking it one step further, there are newsrooms now that are themselves a part of universities or independent nonprofits that are headquartered at universities. However, while many successful investigations have resulted from such internal collaborations, it must be recognized that a university may exert fiscal and political pressure on one of those newsrooms if the investigation is about the university itself. In addition, journalists often find that university professors and staff are used to a slower pace and extended deadlines that are months or years ahead, which could hinder journalism timelines.


## Nonprofit and For-Profit Newsrooms

While many collaborations are happening on a larger scale, they are also possible within a smaller community with help from different types of resources and collaborators. The Center for Cooperative Journalism, which keeps track of journalism collaborations and writes about them, did a profile of an investigation into concussions suffered by high school athletes in Oregon. According to the Center's article on the collaboration, John Schrag, executive editor of a group of Oregon community newspapers, knew that data on medical care for athletes suffering concussions in high school sports was required by state law, but that the data has not been gathered or analyzed. To get the information, however, the data would have to be collected from 238 high schools. As a result, Schrag reached out to Lee van der Voo of the nonprofit newsroom InvestigateWest, who had overseen data-driven projects, and Emily Harris at "Reveal" for their help in collecting and contextualizing the data.

The team also included the University of Oregon's Agora Journalism Center, tapping into its expertise in engagement to help involve members of the local community in the project, particularly high school teachers, students and parents. In addition, the Center also had training for young reporters on how to collect and analyze the data and included a volunteer researcher from Portland State University to review statistical conclusions of the stories.

When about half of the schools had responded to requests for data, the team decided to publish stories showing the frequency and dangers of the concussions. The collaboration resulted in a model for how smaller for-profit newsrooms, nonprofit newsrooms and universities can work together and pool their resources to create a much larger story.


## Crowdsourcing

Another model for collaborative journalism involves crowdsourcing --- or taking information from a wide variety of people or organizations to get a fuller picture of a problem or issue. Crowdsourcing can often involve harnessing the power of a news organization's audience. For example, many news organizations put out calls on social media for members of the public who have had specific experiences in order to get a larger scope of a more widespread issue. The Houston Chronicle found many examples for its investigation of failing special education in Texas by using Facebook to ask parents who were having problems in their school districts to contact the newspaper. This allowed the journalists to reach a much wider group of people that they may not have known, or known how to contact in the first place.

There is also a model of crowdsourcing using other media organizations. Working with both professional and student media, newsrooms nationwide have been harnessed to gather examples, reach out to their own audiences and look for patterns that would be missed by individual news organizations working alone. ProPublica has used this approach on several occasions, on projects examining everything from hate crimes to voter suppression at the polls on Election Day.

Rachel Glickhouse of ProPublica offered several recommendations for conducting such projects in an IRE tip sheet. Glickhouse's recommendations when considering who to partner with involve thinking about:

- Who is best equipped to report these tips out?
- Who serves the audiences you want to reach?
- Which newsrooms have journalists willing to participate?
- Who do you already know that you could work with?
- What kind of stories will this effort likely produce?

When looking at the stories that can be produced using this kind of collaborative sourcing, Glickhouse lists options such as:

- Reporting individual tips as stand-alone stories. This is particularly useful when working with local media outlets.
- Identifying patterns in the data and reporting as many tips as possible that are part of that pattern.
- Using the database that is compiled to identify sources for stories you're already working on.

In the end, Glickhouse and many other journalists who work collaboratively come back to the same general principle for successful partnerships, regardless of the type of project or the number of partners involved: Trust.

- :::
